audit_notes: v3 strict-provenance rewrite. Task is _status: no_canonical (stub) and the visible instruction is the single sentence 'Can you see the PR we have on this repo?'. The substantive intent surface is therefore the user-simulator trajectory + oracle_intents.json (42 intents), NOT an oracle diff. Every goal in v3 must cite a specific intent_id (or instruction text) as its provenance and must be phrased behaviorally — no oracle-only identifiers, no count cliffs that the user did not personally state, no F2P-test-derived sub-check tallies smuggled in as goal copy. The git-ops anti-cheat gate inside test.sh independently handles the `git checkout origin/main -- .` exploit and is not modeled here.

completeness_goals.goal: Agent produces a substantive, on-topic body of work touching the VibeComfy repository (cli_tools/, .claude/skills/, README, dependency files, tests/, or .mcp.json). No-op responses, refusals, pure-question replies ('this PR is unclear, please clarify'), or off-topic edits to wholly unrelated subtrees do NOT satisfy this gate. As a concrete floor: the agent's final diff must edit or create at least one file under one of {cli_tools/, .claude/skills/, tests/, .mcp.json, requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, README.md}. Mass-creating files in an orthogonal subtree (e.g., generating an entire parallel package outside cli_tools/ that does not import from or interact with cli_tools.registry / cli_tools.analysis) does NOT count — the work must demonstrably target the surfaces the user pointed at.
provenance: instruction.md: 'Can you see the PR we have on this repo?' — implies engagement with the PR's actual surface (cli_tools / skills / MCP), not a fabricated parallel one
intent_5 (request): 'Yes, tell me details' — user expects engagement with the PR content
intent_7 (request): 'think through how to integrate precisely and then i'll give the goahead' — user wants execution after planning

anti-gaming: needed because trial vibecomfy-mcp-pr-integration__YDYKZF4 produced a 581k-line patch that recreated an unrelated parallel package and scored 0 on every behavioral goal despite massive volume
completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Pure volume does not satisfy this gate. A diff that adds 500 files entirely outside the surfaces the user pointed at (cli_tools/, .claude/skills/, README, tests/, deps, .mcp.json) fails. Edits to harbor_tasks/, tests/test.sh, install_config.json, .git/, or other harness/eval surfaces are excluded from the count.

completeness_goals.goal: Factor the query-expansion / alias data out of cli_tools/registry/knowledge.py into a separately-importable Python module that knowledge.py then imports. The new module exports (a) some alias-table data structure mapping concept keys (e.g., upscaling, sampling, conditioning/controlnet, video, latent ops) to synonym lists, AND (b) a query-expansion callable that, given an input term, returns an iterable containing the original term plus synonyms when the term (or one of its alias values) matches a known concept. The expansion callable must not raise on empty string or unknown input. knowledge.py must import from this new module rather than defining the alias table inline. Identifier names (module path, dict name, function name) are free — any reasonable choice that satisfies the behavior counts. Substantive coverage means the alias table addresses multiple ComfyUI workflow concepts (the rough floor is ~10+ distinct concept keys covering at least three of: upscaling/super-resolution, sampling, conditioning or controlnet, video, latent operations); the exact count is not material and was never stated by the user.
provenance: intent_7 (request): 'think through how to integrate precisely and then i'll give the goahead'
intent_8 (request): 'Why not both, We should update it directly, Anything worth keeping?' — directly licenses factoring + updating in place
intent_14 (question): 'What does that TASK ALIASES thing even do?' — confirms the alias surface is part of the integrated artifact
intent_15 (question): 'Is it sensible?' — quality / sense-check expectation
completeness_goals.anti_gaming: A stub dict with one or two entries does not satisfy the substantive-coverage clause. Re-exporting an empty dict from a new module while leaving the real table inline in knowledge.py fails (knowledge.py must actually import and use the new module's data path). A module that 'exists' but is never imported by knowledge.py fails.

completeness_goals.goal: Wire cli_tools/analysis (workflow analysis) into the MCP server (cli_tools/registry/mcp_server.py) so the new analysis capabilities are exposed as MCP tools. The MCP server module must (a) import from or otherwise call into cli_tools.analysis (directly or via a thin shim), (b) register at least two NEW MCP tools beyond the pre-existing search/spec/list/stats family — the new tools cover analysis-flavored concerns (any of: upstream-dependency lookup, downstream-dependent lookup, path/trace between nodes, full workflow analysis, subgraph extraction), (c) provide actionable tool descriptions (a downstream agent reading the description can decide when to call the tool — not just a one-word label or a function-name echo), and (d) leave the module importable (no syntax errors, no missing symbols on import). The specific analysis function names referenced are free; the requirement is that the MCP surface gains the analysis capability, not that any specific function be named.
provenance: instruction.md: PR #1 head whose body the agent is asked to engage with adds MCP + analysis wiring
intent_7 (request): 'think through how to integrate precisely and then i'll give the goahead'
intent_8 (request): 'Why not both, update it directly' — explicitly licenses merging analysis into the MCP server
intent_23 (question): 'how does the MCP server play into this' — confirms MCP is the integration surface
intent_24 (request): 'think through how they all work together - what's the path?' — confirms cross-module wiring expectation
intent_39 (question): 'Anything about the mcp server that needs to be updated?' — verification touchpoint
completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Adding a no-op `import cli_tools.analysis` with zero new tool registrations fails (need actual tools). Two new tool entries whose handler bodies never reference analysis functions fail (the import must be load-bearing). A description that is a single word or a verbatim copy of the function name does not count as 'actionable'. Tools registered in a sibling file that mcp_server.py does not pick up fail.

completeness_goals.goal: Add a test suite under tests/ (or an equivalent pytest-discoverable layout at the repo root) that covers the new alias/expansion surface AND the analysis integration. At least one test references the alias or query-expansion surface (by importing from the new shared module OR by exercising knowledge.py's expanded search behavior). At least one test references at least one analysis-flavored capability (any of the analysis function names exposed in cli_tools/analysis, or the new MCP analysis tools exercised through their handler). pytest must be able to COLLECT the tests without errors (collection-only success is the floor; full pass adds confidence but is not strictly required, because missing test-time deps in the eval env should not penalize an agent that wrote correct tests).
provenance: intent_10 (question): 'Do we have tests already?' — establishes test-suite as a concern
intent_11 (request): 'create a test for every tool function that you as an agent can sense-check' — direct request, primary anchor for this goal
intent_12 (question): 'do the test responses all make sense to you?' — qualitative-coverage expectation
intent_40 (verification): 'run all the tests' — final-step verification confirms tests are expected to exist and run
completeness_goals.anti_gaming: A single test file that is empty or contains only `def test_nothing(): pass` fails. A test file that imports the alias module but only asserts that it exists (without exercising expansion behavior) is weak but acceptable; a test that asserts nothing at all is not. Tests that mock out the analysis layer so heavily that they never call any analysis surface fail the analysis-reference clause.

completeness_goals.goal: Reorganize .claude/skills/ so it is no longer a single all-encompassing skill. Acceptable outcomes (any one is sufficient): (a) at least 2 distinct SKILL.md files under .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md with non-overlapping concerns and proper frontmatter (a 'name:' field plus a 'description:' field containing trigger phrasing about when to invoke the skill); (b) the original monolithic skill is preserved but augmented by at least one clearly-scoped sibling sub-skill with its own SKILL.md and frontmatter. The user's correction in intent_30 ('are our skills not very broad right now?') is the binding constraint — penalize a single all-encompassing skill, but do NOT pin to '3+' or any specific count, since the user never stated one. Skills must direct agents toward this repo's tools (search/create/edit nodes and workflows) per intent_22, not be generic boilerplate.
provenance: intent_17 (question): 'Does the stuff in SKILLs feed into what we have or orthogonal' — opens the skill reorg phase
intent_22 (request): 'use the tools in this repo to actually like search things and create nodes' — direct content guidance for what skills should direct agents to do
intent_28 (question): 'is it good and descriptive for what we need? And should the skills be more broken down' — direct prompt to subdivide
intent_30 (question): 'are our skills not very broad right now?' — binding correction, the load-bearing intent
intent_32 (question): 'Is the content of those skills good?' — quality expectation
completeness_goals.anti_gaming: Creating two SKILL.md files with the same content under different names fails the 'non-overlapping concerns' clause. A SKILL.md with no frontmatter or with only a 'name:' field and no 'description:' fails. A skill description that is a one-line generic stub ('describes skills') fails the trigger-phrasing expectation. Sub-skills with no real content (under ~10 substantive lines) fail the augmentation clause.

completeness_goals.goal: Address the two deployment/discovery follow-ups the user explicitly raised. (A) MCP discoverability: at least one of (i) .mcp.json at the repo root with an mcpServers entry that references the new server module, (ii) claude_desktop_config.json with an equivalent mcpServers block referencing the new server module, OR (iii) pyproject.toml with a server-registration section (e.g., [tool.mcp] or a console_scripts entrypoint that the README documents as the MCP launch path). (B) Dependency tracking: the 'mcp' Python package is declared as a project dependency in at least one of requirements.txt, pyproject.toml [project.dependencies], setup.py install_requires, uv.lock, or pdm.lock. Both sub-goals A and B must be satisfied for full credit; either alone earns partial.
provenance: intent_27 (question): 'And what about the MCP?' — direct prompt for MCP discoverability config
intent_36 (question): 'any requirements we need?' — direct prompt for dependency tracking
intent_39 (question): 'Anything about the mcp server that needs to be updated?' — final verification touchpoint
completeness_goals.anti_gaming: An .mcp.json that contains only `{}` or `{"mcpServers":{}}` fails (must reference the server module). Declaring 'mcp' as a comment in pyproject.toml does not count — must be in a real dependency stanza or lockfile entry. Listing 'mcp-server-anything' that does not name 'mcp' or 'mcp[cli]' as the actual installable package fails the dep clause.

completeness_goals.goal: Update the README.md so it explains how to use the integrated MCP / analysis surface. At minimum the README must (a) mention at least one of the new MCP tool names introduced in goal_2, OR (b) include a short example invocation block (a code fence) that shows how to launch / register the MCP server, OR (c) add example prompts after the existing intro section as the user explicitly requested in intent_34 (one example per category is sufficient). The README does not need to be extensively rewritten — a focused, on-topic addition satisfies this goal.
provenance: intent_31 (question): 'Should we update the README?'
intent_33 (question): 'What's the top of the README.md like?' — read-then-edit expectation
intent_34 (request): 'Put example prompts after the intro but just put one example per type' — direct request, binding for the example-prompts variant
intent_35 (request): 'could that readme be tighten up?' — quality / tightness expectation
completeness_goals.anti_gaming: A README addition that is a single new line ('Now with MCP!') fails substantive-mention. Example prompts that are placeholders (`<your prompt here>`) without concrete content fail. Pure formatting changes (whitespace, header reordering) with no new MCP/analysis content fail.

decomposition_notes: Weights: 0.06 (anti-effort) + 0.27 + 0.27 + 0.14 + 0.12 + 0.09 + 0.05 = 1.00. Two cores (alias-factoring + MCP-analysis wiring) carry the most weight because they are the headline integration ask anchored in intents 7, 8, 23, 24 ('think through how to integrate', 'use both approaches, update directly', 'how does the MCP server play into this'). Four secondaries cover the multi-turn user follow-ups: tests (intent_11, the most direct request), skills reorg (intent_30, the binding correction), MCP-discovery+deps (intents 27, 36, 39), and README (intents 31-35). Anti-effort gate at 0.06 keeps the corpus honest against trials that refuse the under-specified instruction OR produce massive orthogonal patches like trial __YDYKZF4 (581k-line patch outside cli_tools/ that scored 0 on every behavioral goal). Weights are deliberately distinct from F2P numeric weights in tests/test.sh (which use 0.10/0.12/0.18/0.10/0.13/0.10/0.08/0.07 plus upstream 0.20+0.20) — the rubric is not a recapitulation of the test-script weighting and must not be conflated with it.

ground_truth_sources: harbor_tasks/vibecomfy-mcp-pr-integration/instruction.md (single sentence: 'Can you see the PR we have on this repo?')
ground_truth_NOT_used: oracle_session.jsonl assistant content — _status: no_canonical, no diffable oracle
reference_patch.json — stripped_export stub, no patch content
tests/test.sh F2P/P2P weights and sub-check tallies — these are scoring implementation, not user-stated requirements
vibecomfy upstream PR #1 file lists or actual patch — not accessible to the agent at run time and not part of the rubric's grounding

intents_intentionally_not_modeled.intent_0_intent_1: install + run — trivial environment setup, not a benchmark signal
intents_intentionally_not_modeled.intent_2_intent_3: context-only ('I've already logged in') — no work to do
intents_intentionally_not_modeled.intent_6: exploratory question, satisfied by goals 1+2 covering integration
intents_intentionally_not_modeled.intent_9_intent_12_intent_29_intent_32: verification/sense-check meta-asks, partially modeled by anti-gaming clauses in goals 1, 2, 4
intents_intentionally_not_modeled.intent_13_intent_18_intent_19_intent_20_: discussion / explain / hypothetical questions, not actionable deliverables
intents_intentionally_not_modeled.intent_16_intent_38_intent_41: git/PR/branch operations — simulator-skipped per user_simulation_prompt.md rule 3 ('skip PR/git operations')
intents_intentionally_not_modeled.intent_37: file-location question, satisfied indirectly by goal_1 (factoring into a separate module is itself a placement decision)

oracle_identifiers_avoided: TASK_ALIASES (literal) — softened to 'some alias-table data structure'
specific module path (cli_tools.search, cli_tools.registry.task_aliases, etc.) — softened to 'a separately-importable Python module'
analyze_workflow / find_upstream / find_downstream / find_path (literal) — softened to 'analysis function names referenced are free'
'25+ alias entries' — softened to '~10+ distinct concept keys covering at least three concept families'
'3+ skills' — softened to '≥2 distinct SKILL.md OR augmented monolith with sub-skill'
.mcp.json as the only acceptable surface — broadened to {.mcp.json, claude_desktop_config.json, pyproject.toml [tool.mcp] or entrypoint}
exact dependency-file location — broadened to {requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, setup.py, uv.lock, pdm.lock}

checks.name: every_goal_cites_specific_intent_id_or_instruction
checks.note: All 7 goals carry a provenance[] array with intent_id citations.

checks.name: no_goal_uses_oracle_only_identifier_as_requirement
checks.note: v3 nowhere requires TASK_ALIASES / expand_query / specific module path / specific analysis function name.

checks.name: no_count_cliff_user_did_not_state
checks.note: v2's '25+ aliases' and '3+ skills' cliffs are softened. The remaining '2 new MCP tools' floor in goal_2 is the count the user implicitly authorizes by saying 'use both approaches' across two integration surfaces (upstream + downstream / path / analyze); kept as a minimum because <2 means the integration didn't happen.

checks.name: weights_sum_to_1_and_distinct_from_test_sh_weights
checks.note: Sum=1.00. Per-goal weights (0.06/0.27/0.27/0.14/0.12/0.09/0.05) deliberately do not mirror F2P/P2P numeric weights in tests/test.sh (0.10/0.12/0.18/0.10/0.13/0.10/0.08/0.07).

checks.name: goal_0_anti_effort_present
checks.note: goal_0 floors out trivial trials AND specifically guards against the orthogonal-massive-patch failure mode observed in trial __YDYKZF4.

checks.name: anti_gaming_clause_in_every_goal
checks.note: Every goal has an anti_gaming field with at least one concrete failure mode listed.

checks.name: behavioral_phrasing_throughout
checks.note: All goals describe observable behavior (imports, callable surfaces, file existence, content, frontmatter shape) rather than internal-state checks an agent could only satisfy by name-matching an oracle.

checks.name: no_dependence_on_oracle_diff
checks.note: Task is _status: no_canonical; rubric grounds entirely on intents + instruction; no oracle patch content is referenced as a requirement.

checks.name: judge_can_decide_pass_fail_without_running_tests
checks.note: Each goal's behavioral clause is judgeable from the agent's diff + a single import / file-content inspection. The rubric does not require running pytest to judge — pytest collection is a floor in goal_3, but rubric-judging is decoupled from test.sh execution.

first_pass_issues_found_and_fixed: Initial v3 draft inherited v2's 'at least one well-known alias key (upscale, sampler, controlnet, lora)' phrasing — softened in goal_1 to 'concept keys covering at least three concept families' to avoid pinning the alias namespace to specific words the user never enumerated.
Initial v3 draft put .mcp.json as primary surface and other discovery paths as 'fallbacks'; revised to a true OR set so judge does not silently downrank claude_desktop_config.json submissions.

Initial v3 draft had goal_6 (README) at weight 0.10; lowered to 0.05 because intent_31 is a question ('Should we update the README?') and intent_34's example-prompts request is narrower than a full README rewrite — keeping it modest avoids overweighting follow-up polish vs the cores.

Initial v3 anti-effort gate (goal_0) was generic; tightened to specifically require edits under the surfaces the user pointed at (cli_tools / skills / README / deps / .mcp.json / tests) after reviewing trial __YDYKZF4 where a 581k-line patch missed every one of these.

Verified anti-gaming clauses do not accidentally require oracle-specific identifiers — each clause names failure modes (empty dict, no-op import, single-word description) without naming the 'correct' value.

_dropped_from_v2.v2_goal: goal_1's '~15+ as floor, qualitative coverage of main concepts' phrasing
_dropped_from_v2.v3_change: softened to '~10+ distinct concept keys covering at least three of: upscaling/super-resolution, sampling, conditioning or controlnet, video, latent operations'
_dropped_from_v2.reason: The 15-entry floor was still a number the user never stated. The concept-family framing is closer to the actual user intent ('does it cover ComfyUI workflow basics') and is judgeable without counting.

_dropped_from_v2.v2_goal: goal_4's 'b) augmented monolith' and 'c) per-concern restructure' triple option
_dropped_from_v2.v3_change: consolidated to two options: (a) ≥2 distinct SKILL.md OR (b) original + ≥1 sibling sub-skill
_dropped_from_v2.reason: Option (c) overlapped with (a) and was a curator artifact, not a user-stated alternative. Two options match intent_30's binary 'are skills too broad' framing.

_dropped_from_v2.v2_goal: goal_5's '~15+ alias entries is the floor' threshold tucked inside goal_1
_dropped_from_v2.v3_change: removed from goal_5 entirely (goal_5 is now MCP discovery + deps only); the alias-coverage floor lives in goal_1
_dropped_from_v2.reason: Mixing surfaces in one goal made the rubric harder to judge; cleaner separation per concern.

_added_for_coverage.reason: v2 did not model intents 31, 33, 34, 35 (README questions and explicit example-prompts request). These were sustained late-session user concerns and should be partially credited. Weight kept modest (0.05) because they are follow-ups, not the integration core.

_added_for_coverage.v3_addition: goal_0 specifically calls out 'mass-creating files in an orthogonal subtree' as a failure mode
_added_for_coverage.reason: Trial __YDYKZF4 demonstrates the exact failure pattern: a 581k-line patch that ignored cli_tools/ and built a parallel package elsewhere. Without this anti-gaming clause, a similar trial could pass goal_0 on diff-size alone.

_added_for_coverage.v3_addition: every goal carries a provenance[] array of intent_id citations
_added_for_coverage.reason: v2 had only natural-language rationales; v3 makes the user-ask traceability machine-checkable. The _provenance.intent_id_citations_per_goal block summarizes the cross-reference.
Explicit ledger of identifiers softened away, so any future audit can confirm no oracle-only constraints leaked back in.

Documents that 42-intent-coverage is not the goal — only actionable, deliverable intents become goals. Prevents over-fitting to chat noise (verification questions, hypothetical questions, git ops the simulator skips).

_known_limitations: Because the task is _status: no_canonical, the judge cannot triangulate against a reference diff — only against the agent's diff vs. the rubric. This is a known property of stub tasks, not a v3-specific weakness.

Anti-gaming clauses use natural-language failure modes; a sufficiently adversarial agent could still find loopholes (e.g., minimal-but-technically-compliant tests). The judge is expected to apply common sense when the agent is clearly stub-satisfying rather than substantively engaging.
Goal_5 'A xor B earns partial' is a hint to the judge; the rubric does not prescribe an exact partial-credit fraction since v3 leaves judge discretion intact.
